does: This paper investigates how sparse autoencoders and other dictionary-based explainers lose faithfulness when neural networks encounter out-of-distribution data. The authors identify that distribution shift causes the model to activate a rotated subspace of its internal representations, which misaligns with the explainer's dictionary trained on in-distribution data. They formalize this problem geometrically as a faithfulness gap—the distance between the ID-trained dictionary and the OOD-active subspace—and demonstrate this gap predicts degradation in explanation quality. To address this, they introduce Geometry-Adaptive Explainer (GAE), a method that realigns the dictionary to match the OOD-active subspace without requiring gradient updates, needing only unlabeled OOD activations. Theoretical analysis proves GAE reduces excess loss with bounds proportional to the squared magnitude of moment shift, and experiments show it recovers or exceeds the performance of explainers trained directly on OOD data.
connects: This work extends the recent surge in mechanistic interpretability research that treats neural networks as decomposable systems of interpretable features, building directly on the sparse autoencoder framework popularized for discovering monosemantic features in language models. The paper responds to a critical gap in this literature: while dictionary-based explainers have shown promise for in-distribution interpretation, their robustness properties under distribution shift have been largely unexplored. The geometric perspective on subspace rotation connects to classical work on domain adaptation and transfer learning, which similarly address how model behavior changes across data distributions. The focus on preserving feature structure while adapting to OOD data relates to recent work on invariant representations and causal discovery under shift, which seek explanations robust to environmental changes rather than tied to specific training conditions.
enables: This work opens several future directions for robust mechanistic interpretability. It establishes methodology for adapting explanations to arbitrary OOD distributions without labeled data or retraining, enabling interpretation of models in deployment scenarios where distribution shift is inevitable. The geometric framework for understanding faithfulness degradation could extend to other explanation methods beyond dictionaries, such as attention patterns or concept-based explanations, providing unified principles for robust interpretability. The connection between moment shift and excess loss suggests pathways toward predicting explanation reliability from minimal OOD samples. More broadly, this positions mechanistic interpretability as compatible with real-world deployment where understanding why models behave differently under shift becomes crucial for safety, auditing, and debugging in deployed systems encountering novel data distributions.
